perturbation.original_text: For purposes of this Agreement, “ Confidential Information ” means, with respect to either party, any and all information in written, representational, electronic, verbal or other form relating directly or indirectly to the present or potential business, operation or financial condition of or relating to the disclosing party (including, but not limited to, information identified as being proprietary and/or confidential or pertaining to, pricing, marketing plans or strategy, volumes, services rendered, customers and suppliers lists, financial or technical or service matters or data, employee/agent/ consultant/officer/director related personal or sensitive data and any information which might reasonably be presumed to be proprietary or confidential in nature) excluding any such information which (i) is known to the public (through no act or omission of the receiving party in violation of this Agreement); (ii) is lawfully acquired by the receiving party from an independent source having no obligation to maintain the confidentiality of such information; (iii) was known to the receiving party prior to its disclosure under this Agreement; (iv) was or is independently developed by the receiving party without breach of this Agreement; or (v) is required to be disclosed by governmental or judicial order, in which case the party so required shall give the other party prompt written notice, where possible, and use reasonable efforts to ensure that such disclosure is accorded confidential treatment and also to enable such other party to seek a protective order or other appropriate remedy at such other party’s sole costs.
perturbation.changed_text: For purposes of this Agreement, “ Confidential Information ” means, with respect to either party, any and all information in written, representational, electronic, verbal or other form relating directly or indirectly to the present or potential business, operation or financial condition of or relating to the disclosing party (including, but not limited to, information identified as being proprietary and/or confidential or pertaining to, pricing, marketing plans or strategy, volumes, services rendered, customers and suppliers lists, financial or technical or service matters or data, employee/agent/ consultant/officer/director related personal or sensitive data and any information which might reasonably be presumed to be proprietary or confidential in nature) excluding any such information which (i) is known to the public (through no act or omission of the receiving party); (ii) is lawfully acquired by the receiving party from an independent source; (iii) was known to the receiving party prior to its disclosure under this Agreement; (iv) was or is independently developed by the receiving party without breach of this Agreement; or (v) is required to be disclosed by governmental or judicial order, in which case the party so required shall give the other party prompt written notice, where possible, and use reasonable efforts to ensure that such disclosure is accorded confidential treatment and also to enable such other party to seek a protective order or other appropriate remedy at such other party’s sole costs.
perturbation.explanation: The original text in Section 1 excludes information known to the public 'through no act or omission of the receiving party in violation of this Agreement'. The modified text omits 'in violation of this Agreement'. This in-text contradiction creates uncertainty about what is considered 'Confidential Information'. For example, if the receiving party unintentionally violates the agreement and information becomes public, it's unclear whether that information is still protected under the agreement, as previously defined. This is because the definition of Confidential Information is now broadened, potentially conflicting with other obligations where the assumption is that the Confidential Information has been kept with 'reasonable care'.
perturbation.contradicted_text: The receiving party shall use the same degree of care and protection to protect the Confidential Information received by it from the disclosing party as it uses to protect its own Confidential Information of a like nature, and in no event such degree of care and protection shall be of less than a reasonable degree of care.
perturbation.justification: The original text excludes information known to the public 'through no act or omission of the receiving party in violation of this Agreement' from being considered confidential. The changed text removes 'in violation of this Agreement', broadening the exclusion. This directly contradicts the 'Contradicted Text' which states 'The receiving party shall use the same degree of care and protection to protect the Confidential Information...'. By removing the 'in violation of this agreement' clause, any information known to the public is no longer confidential, regardless of whether it became public due to the receiving party's violation. This means the receiving party doesn't have to protect the information that they caused to be public, directly conflicting with the standard of care required in the contradicted section. This creates a direct conflict and a strong in-text contradiction as it undermines the obligation to protect confidential information.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement does not obligate either party to disclose any particular proprietary information; to purchase, sell, license, transfer, or otherwise dispose of any technology, services, or products; or to enter into any other form of business, contract or arrangement.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement does not obligate either party to disclose any particular trade secret; to purchase, sell, license, transfer, or otherwise dispose of any technology, services, or products; or to enter into any other form of business, contract or arrangement.
perturbation.explanation: In Section 2, replacing 'proprietary information' with 'trade secret' introduces ambiguity. While all trade secrets are proprietary, not all proprietary information constitutes a trade secret. The later clauses of the contract, specifically clause 14, treat 'Confidential Information constituting a trade secret under applicable law' differently, extending confidentiality indefinitely. This change creates an in-text contradiction by potentially narrowing the scope of what isn't obligated to be disclosed, while later clauses have different treatments based on the 'trade secret' qualifier. This impacts the agreement as now disclosing trade secrets is being treated different, and makes the current obligation unclear and not enforceable.
perturbation.contradicted_text: This Agreement shall remain valid for a period of three (3) years from the date of execution of this Agreement which term may be extended by mutual consent in writing of both the parties. This Agreement may be terminated by either party by giving thirty (30) days notice in writing to the other party without assigning any reason whatsoever. The obligations of each party hereunder will continue and be binding irrespective of whether the discussion between the parties materialise into a specific understanding/business relationship or not and for a further period of three (3) years after termination/expiry of the Agreement. In any event, however, the obligations of each Party to maintain the confidentiality of the Confidential Information it has received under this agreement shall continue indefinitely as to Confidential Information constituting a trade secret under applicable law.
perturbation.justification: The change from 'proprietary information' to 'trade secret' in the Changed Text narrows the scope of information not obligated to be disclosed. The Contradicted Text specifies that the confidentiality obligations for 'Confidential Information constituting a trade secret under applicable law' continue indefinitely, while other confidential information has a limited duration. This creates an in-text contradiction because the initial obligation not to disclose is now qualified by 'trade secret', triggering different confidentiality terms in the Contradicted Text. This aligns with the definition of Misaligned Terminology - In Text Contradiction, as the contract uses the terms 'proprietary information' and 'trade secret' without defining the implications of the change.

perturbation.original_text: Each party agrees and undertakes that it shall not, without first obtaining the written consent of the other, disclose or make available to any person, reproduce or transmit in arty manner, or use (directly or indirectly) for its own benefit or the benefit of others, any Confidential Information save and except both parties may disclose any Confidential Information to their Affiliates, directors, officers, employees or advisors of their own or of Affiliates on a “need to know” basis to enable them to evaluate such Confidential Information in connection with the negotiation of the possible business relationship; provided that such persons have been informed of, and agree to be bound by obligations which are at least as strict as the recipient’s obligations hereunder. For the purpose of this Agreement, Affiliates shall mean, with respect to any party, any other person directly or indirectly Controlling, Controlled by, or under direct or indirect common Control with, such party. “Control”, “Controlled” or “Controlling” shall mean, with respect to any person, any circumstance in which such person is controlled by another person by virtue of the latter person controlling the composition of the Board of Directors or owning the largest or controlling percentage of the voting securities of such person or by way of contractual relationship or otherwise.
perturbation.changed_text: Each party agrees and undertakes that it shall not, without first obtaining the oral consent of the other, disclose or make available to any person, reproduce or transmit in arty manner, or use (directly or indirectly) for its own benefit or the benefit of others, any Confidential Information save and except both parties may disclose any Confidential Information to their Affiliates, directors, officers, employees or advisors of their own or of Affiliates on a “need to know” basis to enable them to evaluate such Confidential Information in connection with the negotiation of the possible business relationship; provided that such persons have been informed of, and agree to be bound by obligations which are at least as strict as the recipient’s obligations hereunder. For the purpose of this Agreement, Affiliates shall mean, with respect to any party, any other person directly or indirectly Controlling, Controlled by, or under direct or indirect common Control with, such party. “Control”, “Controlled” or “Controlling” shall mean, with respect to any person, any circumstance in which such person is controlled by another person by virtue of the latter person controlling the composition of the Board of Directors or owning the largest or controlling percentage of the voting securities of such person or by way of contractual relationship or otherwise.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'written consent' to 'oral consent' in Section 3 creates an in-text contradiction regarding enforceability. Section 13 states that 'This Agreement shall not be amended, assigned or transferred by either party without the written consent of the other party.' Granting consent orally contradicts the requirement for written amendments, making it difficult to enforce the confidentiality provisions. Now if the agreement is needed to be amended with an oral consent it won't be valid, which also creates an in-text contradiction.
perturbation.contradicted_text: This Agreement shall not be amended, assigned or transferred by either party without the written consent of the other party.
perturbation.justification: The changed text modifies the original text by replacing 'written consent' with 'oral consent' in Section 3. The contradicted text, taken from Section 13, explicitly states that 'This Agreement shall not be amended, assigned or transferred by either party without the written consent of the other party.' This creates a direct contradiction because Section 3 now allows for oral consent in one instance, while Section 13 mandates written consent for any amendments to the agreement. Therefore, the changed text directly contradicts the contradicted text, leading to uncertainty about the enforceability of the confidentiality provisions and the agreement itself.

perturbation.original_text: The receiving party shall use the same degree of care and protection to protect the Confidential Information received by it from the disclosing party as it uses to protect its own Confidential Information of a like nature, and in no event such degree of care and protection shall be of less than a reasonable degree of care.
perturbation.changed_text: The receiving party shall use its best effort to protect the Confidential Information received by it from the disclosing party as it uses to protect its own Confidential Information, and in no event such effort and protection shall be required to be of a reasonable degree of care.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'same degree of care and protection to protect the Confidential Information received by it from the disclosing party as it uses to protect its own Confidential Information of a like nature, and in no event such degree of care and protection shall be of less than a reasonable degree of care' to 'best effort to protect the Confidential Information received by it from the disclosing party as it uses to protect its own Confidential Information, and in no event such effort and protection shall be required to be of a reasonable degree of care' creates ambiguity and in-text contradiction. Initially, a 'reasonable degree of care' served as a safeguard. Now, with the explicit removal of the reasonable care standard, it becomes unclear what the expected level of protection is, leading to potential disputes over whether sufficient effort has been made. Furthermore, this contradicts section 6 regarding indemnity, as it's now harder to prove a breach due to the vague 'best effort' standard.
perturbation.contradicted_text: The parties agree to indemnify and keep indemnified each other against all loss and damage, which the disclosing party may suffer as a result of any breach of this Agreement by the receiving party; provided always that the disclosing party shall forthwith give written notice to the receiving party of the above loss and damage and satisfactory documentary evidence of such actual loss and damage.
perturbation.justification: The original text establishes a 'reasonable degree of care' as the minimum standard for protecting confidential information. The changed text replaces this with 'best effort' and explicitly states that 'effort and protection shall not be required to be of a reasonable degree of care'. This directly contradicts the indemnity clause (Contradicted Text), as it removes a clear, objective standard (reasonable care) and replaces it with a subjective one ('best effort'), making it significantly harder to prove a breach and claim indemnity, as explained in the Perturbation Explanation. This aligns with the definition of Misaligned Terminology - In Text Contradiction because the change introduces vagueness, conflicting obligations and ultimately contradicts the contradicted text.

perturbation.original_text: Each party warrants that it has the right to make the disclosures under this Agreement. No other warranties are made by either party under this Agreement and all information exchanged under this Agreement is provided “as is”. The disclosing party shall not be in any way responsible for any decisions or commitments made by receiving party in relying on the disclosing party’s Confidential Information.
perturbation.changed_text: Each party warrants that it has the right to make the disclosures under this Agreement. Other warranties can be made by either party under this Agreement and all information exchanged under this Agreement is provided “as is”. The disclosing party shall not be in any way responsible for any decisions or commitments made by receiving party in relying on the disclosing party’s Confidential Information.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'No other warranties are made by either party under this Agreement' to 'Other warranties can be made by either party under this Agreement' introduces an in-text contradiction related to the risk allocation. The original clause intends to limit the disclosing party's liability by disclaiming additional warranties. Introducing the possibility of 'other warranties' creates uncertainty about the scope of liability. This directly contradicts the 'as is' provision, as 'as is' implies no additional warranties, while the modified text permits them. If these warranties were oral this would also contradict clause 13.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Each party warrants that it has the right to make the disclosures under this Agreement. Other warranties can be made by either party under this Agreement and all information exchanged under this Agreement is provided “as is”. The disclosing party shall not be in any way responsible for any decisions or commitments made by receiving party in relying on the disclosing party’s Confidential Information.
perturbation.justification: The changed text modifies the original clause 'No other warranties are made by either party under this Agreement' to 'Other warranties can be made by either party under this Agreement'. This introduces a contradiction because the original text explicitly disclaims any warranties beyond what's stated, while the changed text opens the possibility of additional warranties. This contradicts the 'as is' provision and introduces uncertainty about the scope of liability, creating a direct conflict within the contract. The changed text and contradicted text are in the same location, but the change causes a contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: The parties agree to indemnify and keep indemnified each other against all loss and damage, which the disclosing party may suffer as a result of any breach of this Agreement by the receiving party; provided always that the disclosing party shall forthwith give written notice to the receiving party of the above loss and damage and satisfactory documentary evidence of such actual loss and damage.
perturbation.changed_text: The parties agree to indemnify and keep indemnified each other against all loss and damage, which the disclosing party may suffer as a result of any breach of this Agreement by the receiving party; provided always that the disclosing party shall give notice to the receiving party of the above loss and damage.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the clause 'and satisfactory documentary evidence of such actual loss and damage' from Section 6 creates an in-text contradiction regarding the burden of proof. The original clause mandates 'satisfactory documentary evidence,' setting a clear evidentiary standard for claiming indemnity. Removing this clause makes it unclear what evidence is required to prove 'actual loss and damage', making it potentially easier to claim indemnity without sufficient proof. This may be related to an oral claim of loss, without the proper documentary evidence. This contradicts the intent of the indemnification clause, as it removes a safeguard against frivolous or unsubstantiated claims, it also introduces ambiguity into the enforcement of the agreement's indemnification provision.
perturbation.contradicted_text: The parties agree to indemnify and keep indemnified each other against all loss and damage, which the disclosing party may suffer as a result of any breach of this Agreement by the receiving party; provided always that the disclosing party shall give notice to the receiving party of the above loss and damage.
perturbation.justification: The original text requires 'satisfactory documentary evidence' for indemnity claims, setting a clear evidentiary standard. The changed text removes this requirement, creating a contradiction because it's now unclear what evidence is needed, potentially allowing claims without sufficient proof. This undermines the purpose of the indemnity clause and the standard originally established, and directly conflicts with the intent of the original agreement, creating a strong in-text contradiction with the contradicted text, which is the same as the changed text, now with less protections than initially agreed. This aligns with the definition of Misaligned Terminology, because 'evidence' is now undefined, creating an in-text contradiction.
